HIGH COURT (HIGH COURT OF AUSTRALIA.) D ANOTHER i v AND ON APPEAL FROM THE SUPREME COURT oF NEW SOUTH WALES, life—Releasee not to take beneficially but as trustee—Enlargement of elle releaser—Forfeiture—Residuary devisee—Interest vested not contingent A contingent remainder may be released by deed to the tenant for lf such a release will operate by way of enlargement of the latter's estate not merely by way of extinguishment of the contingent remainder. An instrument, which is capable of operating as a release of such an' made in favour of a person capable of accepting it, will pass the legal estate the releasee although by the terms of the instrument the releasee agree accept it, not for his own benefit, but as trustee for others, fine or by a contract for valuable consideration is good law, and whether interests do not pass by an instrument capable of creating estoppel, es! indenture. 'A testator devised certain lands to his wife for life, and, after her death, his two sons M. and J. for their lives as tenants in common in equal shares, the share of either son dying to go to that son's children, who being should attain the age of twenty-one years, or being daughters should attsit that age or marry, and in default of such issue, to the other children of the testator then living. Certain other lands were devised to J. for limitations in remainder after his death us in the former devise. 'The was devised to M., J., and another upon trust to sell, and to hold the p after payment of certain legacies and expenses, in trust for M.and J. ine shares. 'There was a proviso that (inter alia) if any of the children tenants for life should alienate or attempt to alienate their interest or interes inthe lands so devised the interest of such tenant for life should go a o=bit OF AUSTRALIA. thet, purported to "grant bargain sell alien and release" to M. as trustee ja ee all "the lands and property of whatsoever nature and kind soever Jeoluely and otherwise acquired " by him under the will and all his * estate right ttle and interest" therein to hold to such uses as his wife E., one of the sppeats, should appoint, and in default of appointment for her separate use forlife with remainders over.

After the death of the widow of the testator, and of M., who died without issue, E, exercised her power of appointment in favour of the other appellant. J. having become bankrupt six years after the settlement, all his interests tot then legally disposed of passed to the respondent, as official assignee, but the validity of the settlement itself was not affected. Ie was not disputed on the appeal, that the settlement operated to create u forfeiture of J.'s life estate under the will or that upon the forfeiture the intermediate rents and profits, until one of J.'s children attained the age of twenty-one years, fell into residue, Held, that, as to M.'s moiety of the lands devised to the testator's wife, J., atthe date of the settlement had a contingent remainder in fee as tenant in common with such of the testator's children as should survive M., and a rested remainder as residuary devise in joint tenancy in the event of failure af any of the testator's children to survive M.

That Js interest in M.'s moiety was effectually released by the settlement toM.,, either regarded as tenant for life in remainder expectant on decease, or as joint tenant in remainder under the residuary devise : mother's nd That the accretion or enlargement of his original estate which M. thus scquired was bound in his hands by the trusts of the settlement. Doed, Calkin v. Tombinson, 2 M. &S., 165, and In re Ellenborough ; Towry Law.

Burne, 1 Ch., 697, distinguished. Held further that J.'s interest as residuary devise quoad the property com'rised in the second devise, though it depended upon certain contingencies whether he would ever take anything under it, was not contingent, but @ 'vested interest, and passed under the settlement. Egerton v.
Massey, 3 C.B.N.S., 338, followed. of Walker J., 5 S.R. (N.S.W.), 63; 21 N.S.W. W.N., 213, Teversed on these points. AL from a decision of Walker J. sitting as Judge in Equity.

By his will dated 10th September, 1875, Owen Joseph Caraher, 'who died on 22nd August, 1879, made devises in the following —"T give and devise all those six houses and store and 'tlic situated in Gloucester street in the City of Sydney, also the 'hol hereditaments and premises consisting of four houses situate 'Cumberland street which land was bought by me from William "opher death, Before the widow's death J., by voluntary post-nuptial settle. H.C. or A. 1905, — Cananen Lioyp. H.C. or A. 1905. (Cananer Luoyp.

HIGH COURT Austin Hogan, to my wife Catherine Caraher for her life ithou impeachment of waste, and from and after her deeease T ij the same to my sons Michael Joseph Caraher and John Caraher for their lives and without impeachment of the age of twenty-one years or who being danghters ora dau shall have attained that age or shall have married or shall ma shall be then living and if more than one in equal shares" "TI devise my five houses in Cumberland street: opposite ti watch-house to my son John Edwin Caraher for life withoa impeachment of waste, and after his decease I devise the his child or children who being sons or a son shall have att or shall attain that age or shall have married or shall marry a if more than one such child in equal shares, and in default of am such issue as aforesaid then I devise the same to the others other of my children who shall be then living and if more th one in equal shares." : "And as to the rest residue and remainder of my real personal estate I give and devise the same to James Mullens Pitt Street, Sydney . . . my son the said John Edwin Caraher and Edward Flanagan of George Street Sydney « (hereinafter designated trustees) their heirs, executors will. Out of the proceeds the trustees were to pay the funeral and testamentary expenses, and debts, and certain and to stand possessed of the residue, after payment of cert John Edwin Caraher in equal shares. The will also contained the following proviso: "Provided always and I hereby declare that it shall not) lawful for any of my said children hereby made tenants for] of the said premises hereinbefore devised to charge all or any © OF AUSTRALIA. he said prem vest of any sum or sums of money nor in anywise to dispose of any estate or interest in the said premises so hereinbefore devised, nj in the event of the interest of any tenant for life being tached alienated or disposed of by operation of law then I give devise the interest of such tenant for life to the person or enons who would be entitled thereto if such tenant for life were cally dead." Bya codicil dated 11th August, 1879, Michael Joseph Caraher appointed executor and trustee in place of James Mullens 'who had died.

The testator was survived by his wife and eight children, iduding John Edwin and Michael Joseph, and probate was sated to the executors named in the will in September, 1879. 'Thewidow died on 3rd March, 1893. 'The son Michael Joseph 4 on 31st December, 1894, never having married, and the Flanagan in October, 1900, and no person was appointed trustee in place of them. John Edwin Caraher was married in July, 1880, to the appellant, Emily Caraher, and there was issue of themarriage one child, Ethel May Caraher, born in October, 1881. By a voluntary post-nuptial settlement dated 20th January, 1882, John Edwin conveyed to Michael Joseph Caraher as trustee 'nfe all his interests of whatsoever nature and kind under the 'ill upon such trusts as the settlor's wife should by deed or will point and in default of appointment in trust for the wife for life her separate estate, and after her death without having printed in trust for the children of the marriage who should tain the age of twenty-one or marry.

The operative words and imitations of the settlement are more fully set out in the judgwent, No other children were born of the marriage. In August, the estate of John Edwin Caraher was sequestrated in bankuptey, and the respondent was appointed official assignee of the 'siate, which has never been released. In 1894 the marriage of lohn Edwin was dissolved by decree of the Divorce Court, and he Tasnot re-married.

In 1904 the appellant Emily Caraher exertied her power of appointment under the settlement in favour of the other appellant. By notification of resumption dated 29th December, 1900, the 483, ises so hereinbefore devised for life with the pay1. ©. or A. 1905, S Camaner a Luovn. H.C. oF A. 1905.

CaRanER Luoyp. HIGH COURT lands, comprised in the devises set out above, were Crown under the provisions of the Public Works Act 190) the Darting Harbour Wharves Resumption Act 190, axa compensation moneys, with interest, paid into Court. by th Minister. John Edwin Caraher was at this time still living. 'The several claimants, including the appellants, petitioned Court for payment out to them of their respective shares of th moneys paid into Court.

The appellants claimed to be enti under the settlement of 1882, to John Edwin Caraher's in under the will, which was at the date of the settlement contin on Michael John dying before him, and without leaving issne should fulfil the conditions of majority or marriage. There dent contended that the settlement did not operate as an axig ment of this interest, and that therefore it had passed on bau ruptey to the official assignee. Walker J. who heard the petitia sitting as the Judge in Equity, was of opinion thatat law noi passed by the settlement, and that, the settlement being volunt and the property purported to be settled never having come in the hands of the trustee, it was inoperative also in Equity held therefore that the title of the assignee must prevail. also was of opinion that the same reasoning applied to the of John Edwin, as one of the residuary devisees, in the rents 0 the property devised to himself for life in the interval betwee the forfeiture of his life interest and the attainment of maj by his daughter Ethel May: Ex parte the Minister for Works ; In the matter of the Will of Owen Caraher (1).

From this decision the present appeal was brought. Loxton for the appellants, The interest of John Edwin in Michael's share of the property held by them as tenants i common, which was expectant on Michael's death, passe the settlement. It was one of those contingencies or possi which, though incapable of being conveyed to a stranger otftt wise than by estoppel, fine and recovery, or assignment for in Equity, are capable of being voluntarily released to pa who stand in a certain relationship to the releasor, ¢g.t0# 13. (1) 5 S.R. (N.S.W.), 63; 21.N.S.W.

W. OF AUSTRALIA, accepting such a ioe The Sdilenore ea amongst things, to release to Michael all that John had acquired orby virtue of the will. The fact that Michael was to astrustee did not militate against the efficacy of the convance, The rule is one of law, subject to certain exceptions, rnd the Court, in applying it, con only take cognizance of legal ests, If a particular assignment comes within the exceptions, ye Court must enforce the legal title, irrespective of any equities tint may be created at the same time. 'The rule against alienation ofsuch interests was laid down in Lamypet's Case (1).
Anexception s recognized in the case of releases to persons already having an «tate in the land: Thomas v. Freeman (2); Higden v. Williamson Wright v.
Wright (4); In ve Parsons ; Stockley v. Parsons (5); mre Bllenborough; Yowry Law v. Burne (6); Marks v.

Marks f), The reason given in the old authorities for the exeeption in the case of releases was the same as the reason given for nile itself, viz., that it tended to the quieting of disputes. A release was permitted because it resulted in the extinguishment ofan adverse claim, or the enlargement of the estate of the terretenant or remainderman. But, though this was the foundation the exception, it must be applied irrespectively of its origin hether the particular application will result in extinguishment: not.

In the present case the release removed the possibility of disputes, whatever differences might arise between the dhimants of equitie: Anvlease of a possibility is good where the person to whom it ports to be made has the freehold in deed or in law, and therefiw it is good when made to a life tenant : Cruise's Digest of the ws of England, vol. u., pp. 78, 82; Co. Litt, Bk. 11, Hs 448, 447, et seq. ; Williams's Law of Real Property, 9th ed., P22. A release may operate either by extinguishment or as an alargement : Watkins on Conveyancing, 8th ed., p.243; Co.
Litt, : ML, sees. 452, 453, 465-467. Here the release extinguished 828 (48a), (5) 45 Ch. 563, (6) (190: 8 8, 132 (7) 1 Ste., 129 ; 2 Blac. Com., 290. os ee, Caramen Luv.
H.C. or A. 1905. — Cananer v Luoyp. HIGH COURT [Grirrrr C.J. referred to Go. Litt, Bk. 11, see. $05, and pp v.

Dean, (1)] Another consequence of John's attempted alienation of thei estates given him by the will was forfeiture. [He refema Blackman v. Fysh (2); In ve Porter; Coulson v, Cipper gy In ve Tancred's Settlement; Somerville v. Tanered (4) interest in the residuary estate passed to Michael, 'That i was not contingent.

As residuary devisee he had an ulti vested remainder in fee, subject to a contingency. 'This interest expectant upon Michael's death a mere expectaney, was a possibility coupled with an interest. In Jn re Bilenloro Towry Law v. Burne (5), which was relied upon in the below, the subject-matter of the assignment was a mere 4 suecessionis.
A possibility coupled with an interest has al been assignable: Watkins on Conveyancing, 8th ed, p Doe d. Perry v. Jones (6); Shep.

Touch., p. 239. The settlement therefore operated in Michael's favour by way of enlargement d his life estate, and enured to his benefit as ultimate remainderman, he being the sole residuary devisee: Williams on Lew of Ra Property, 9th ed., p. 256. [Grirritn C.J. referred to Jurman on Wills, 5th ed., p Tai) Whatever the operation of the release, John ceased to have interest in the estate, and none therefore passed to the respont the official assignee, on the bankruptcy. The settlement was it 1882 and therefore was not affected by the Bankruptey St Michael took the conveyance under such circumstances as wot lead the Court of Equity to declare that he held the property whit passed under it, subject to the trusts mentioned in the deed. settlor having done everything possible to effectuate the settle the Court will enforce it, although it was voluntary: "Ellison ¥.
Manning (9). ; Dr. Cullen K.C., and Peden for the respondent. It is eone (2 ag9n 3 0 30; 3 LR, 88 (2) 3 (3) 3 Ch.

RRS IS (4) 1 Gi, TB. (5) 1 Ch, come eases a possibility may be released, but in the present ease » settlement did not operate to convey the interest of the xitlor by way of release or othery A release of a possibility nly operate by way of extinguishment, so as to benefit the whose interest would be impaired by the realization of the bility. Whether the interest of the settlor is regarded as an -executory devise or as a contingent remainder, it could not be released to Michael. The former class of interests can only be sd to the person whose estate is liable to be cut down by arising of the interest, and the latter only to the reversioner "whose estate might be prevented from coming into existence by "the happening of the contingency: Williams on Real Property, "Ith ed, p. 357.

Such a release cannot enlarge the interest of the "tenant for life, because his estate must be completed independently the interest of the releasor. Nothing was conveyed to Michael ty the settlement. Michael was in the same position as any mmger, and could only take a conveyance of John's interest by and recovery, or its modern substitute, or by assignment for value in equity. It is only in that way that such a possibility an be passed so as to become an interest in the hands of the transferee. [He referred to Weule v.

Lower (1); Fearne OR, "Thed, p.365; Williams, Seisin of the Freehold, p. 124; Story, Buity Jurisprudence, p. 690 (sec. 1040); Lampet's Case (2); Hyde v. Purrat (3).] John had no vested interest to. convey. residuary devise was contingent upon the failure of all the jects limited, and therefore might never have taken effect. {He referred to Jurman on Wills, 5th ed., vol. 1, p. 757; Steph. Com, 7th ed., vol. 1,, pp. 326, 327.] [Gurerrn C.J. referred to Hyerton vy.

Mussey (4). 'That. case 'seems to show that there was a vested interest in Michael and 'ohn, as to the residue.] No estate after a contingent limitation in fee can be vested: Tuidington v, Kime (5); Vick v. Edwards (6). John's interest ss residuary devisee was consequent upon his own forfeiture. (4) 3C.BN.S., 338 (5) 1 La.
Hackman (2); Wilkinson v. Wilkinson (3); Elphinstone Conveyancing, 5th ed., p. 339. Michael was not tenant for of the estate given to John for life; he was only tenant for life gf his own moiety.

If his capacity to take a release only atuse by virtue of the settlement, he could not take under the se in that capacity. The owner of one possibility cannot release the owner of another. Michael and John, as joint resida devisees, were each possessed of an interest to the extent of moiety, and consequently.

John's interest only affected Michael interest in the whole to the extent of one-half. But a person is capable of taking a release only when his whole interest is liable to be cut down by the coming into effect of the contingeney on which the estate of the releasor depends. (Grier C.J.—A release might have a twofold effect, enlarging — one estate by the extinguishment of another.] A gift to the survivors of several persons is a mere eontingeney_ and ie not coupled with an interest. None of the persons may — survive the tenant for life. [He referred to Preston on Convey aneing, 1818 ed., p. 95; Vick v.
Edwards (4); Fearne, CR, 7th ed., p. 547 ; Doe d. Perry v. Jones (5); Watkins on Conveyancing, Pp. "homas v.
Trevor (9); In re Parsons; Stockley ¥. Parsons (10); In re Ellenborough; Towry Law v. Burne (1); Meck v.

Tomkinson ag; Thomas v. Jones (15); Jarman on Wills, 5th ed., p. 117] [Barron J. referred to Jarman on Wills, 5th ed., p. 49.) The settlement was not a release at all in the sense contended for by the appellants, but an ordinary conveyance by the statutory — (1) 3 Ch., 209, (9) 2P. Wins, 191. (10) 45 Ch.
Wins., 574 (note). (15) 1 De G., J. &S., 08. (8) 2P. Wms., 182, OF AUSTRALIA. OLR} teing by deed acknowledged, or for value, it was inoperative to 'ass acontingent interest.

But assuming that it was a release, "god that Michael was a person capable of accepting one, it was imperative upon the interest in question, whatever effect it may jaye had upon John's other property. A release can only operate asanenlargement where the estate of the releasee is complete ant vested. [He referred to Lampet's Case (1); Mozeley and Whiteley's Law Dictionary, p. 445, definition of terre-tenunt; Sisph. Com, Tth ed., vol. 1, p. 518, and Co.

Litt, Bk. m1, sees. 905, 447, and 450, as to the ways in which a release can operate at Jaw.) Assuming that the release was effective, the result, whether regarded as a release to Michael as one of several joint tenants, or asthe tenant for life, was that the interests released fell into the inheritance: Co. Litt., Bk. 111, sees. 306, 307. That was vested in John, Michael and Flanagan, and therefore ultimately fell to John ly survivorship.

John therefore has not rid himself of the legal tate, and the settlement, being voluntary, will not be enforeed against the settlor. Arelease can only operate by merging the estate of the releasor in the estate of the releasee, if large enough to take it, or in the inheritance, not by preserving the interest released as a separate entity. The fee could not sink in the life tenancy, and Michael cannot have an estate expectant on his own death, for that would make the release operate as a conveyance, not a release, John's interest in Michael's moiety under the first devise therefore sank into the inheritance, as well as his interest in the residue, and the legal estate in both remained in himself.
Tomiinson (3); Fearne, O.R., 9th ed. p. 370; Thomas v. Jones (4); Preston on Conveyancing, 3rd ed., pp. 268, 270, citing Goodright v. Forrester (5); Co.
L, p. 96a. (7) 4 Ves, 631. ivalent of the old lease and release. As such a conveyance, not H.C. or A. 1905. — CaRAHER Luorp. H.C. oF A. 1905. — Laorn.

Sune 18, HIGH COURT The judgment of the Court was read by _GuureimH CJ. 'This is an appeal from an order of Walt of a fund paid into hacen respect of lands taken by the Goveem ment of New South Wales under the Public Works Aet The appellants claim under a voluntary post-nuptial se dated 20th January, 1882, made by one John Edwin Ca 'The respondent claims as official assignee of J. E. Caraher, wh estate was sequestrated on 30th August, 1888.

The questions for determination depend upon the ancient English law of real p perty relating to contingent remainders, which in this not been altered in New South Wales. It will be convenient consider, first, what was the nature of the estate which J. Caraher had in the lands now represented by the fund in Go and, secondly, what, if any, was the effect of his attempted d ing with it by the settlement of 20th January, 1882.

Owen Joseph Caraher, who died on 22nd August, 1879, males will dated 10th September, 1875, which contained devises in t following terms: [His Honor read from the will the devises. proviso already set out, and continued]: By a codicil Michael was substituted for Mullens in the residuary devise. 'The effect of the first devise, stated shortly, was that, after th life estate of the widow, Michael and John Edwin took for life, being tenants in common during their joint lives, remainder in fee as to each moiety for such children of the tenaul such issue to the other children of the testator living at the of the tenant for life as tenants in common in fee. In thept a tions subsequent to his life estate. At the testator's death 3 had no children.

It is clear that the remainder to his eb was contingent: Festing v. Allen (1); and, as it was a re in fee, it follows that the remainder to the testator's children should survive Michael was also contingent. It is also ¢ that this last remainder was a gift to persons in ¢s8¢, aaa : (1) 12M. & W., 279.

LR.) OF AUSTRALIA. yas uneertai "jelas been held by the Supreme Conrt of the United States, falloving the opinion of Chaneellor Kent, that " when an estate js granted to one for life and to such of his children as should be ling after his death the present right to future possession vests atonee in such as are living, subject to open and let in afterborn children and to be divested as to those who shall die without jssne:" Ororall v. Sherard (1). In this view John Edwin's interest was only contingent by reason of its following on the contingent remainder given to Michael's children.

In some of the 'Ameriean States, however, it is held that a remainder is necesily contingent when it is impossible, until the death of the tenant for life, to tell who are entitled under the deseription. Tn the present case it is immaterial which of these views is correet, for in either view the remainder to the children of the testator yascontingent. It was, however, urged before us by the learned counsel for the respondent that the interests of the children of "the testator, if they were contingent remainders at all, were not ofsuch a kind as to be capable at common law of assignment by theassurances appropriate for dealing with contingent remainders, but were mere possibilities not coupled with an interest, and were incapable of being dealt with except by estoppel or contract for valuable consideration ; and the case was likened to the interest of an heir-at-law before the death of the ancestor.

We shall have casio to deal with this argument at greater length. But, in passing, the case of Quarm v. Quarm (2) may be mentioned, In that case a testator had devised a freehold estate to seven persons "asjoint tenants and not as tenants in common and to the survivor of them, his or her heirs and assigns for ever." It was held upon the construction of these words that the devisees were joint tenants for life, with a contingent remainder in fee to the surriver.

The survivor had, twenty years before the contingency was deterinined in his favour, become bankrupt, and it was held that his estate had passed to the trustee in bankruptey. It does tot appear to have occurred to any one concerned in the case to doubt that such an interest passed on bankruptey. It is clear, however, that the expectancy or spes successionis of an heir-at(2) 1 Q.B., 184. in whether all or any of them would take under it, H.C. or A. —— Caraner v.
Laoyn, H.0. oF A. Jaw does not pass to his trustee in bankruptey, if the b 1905. = Caran Lioyp. HIGH COURT is closed before he inherits.

It appears, then, that as to moiety of the lands in question John Edwin had a contin remainder in fee as tenant in common with such of the testa children as should survive Michael. 'This being an esate severalty, it is immaterial that in the events that happened} and those claiming through him only became entitled to om. seventh of Michael's moiety. 'The prineiples governing the g are the same as if they had become entitled to the whole oft the contingency of the failure of the gift to Michael's ehildre Again, the contingent remainder to the children of the te who should survive Michael might have failed altogether, In event the land in question would have fallen into the residuean the legal estate would have vested in the devisees named, viz, Michael, John Edwin, and Flanagan. And this estate was ved and not contingent : Eyerton v. Mussey (1).

At the testata death, therefore, Michael was not only tenant for life of his but was also joint tenant in remainder in fee of the same m We were invited to approach the subject on the assumpti that at common law real property was primd facie inalien and that at any rate contingent remainders could not be alienated But it is, to use the words of Farwell J., "the ancient rule of English law that one of the inseparable incidents of property is the right of alienation by appropriate assurances": Ine Ol Setilement, Evered v. Leigh (2). The learned Judge added th the rule is one of public policy, and that it has always been considered to be the duty of all the Courts to uphold it, not assist in evading it.

The question in each case is as to th appropriate assurance. On this point positive rules were est lished, one of which was said to be that certain kinds of property, including contingent remainders, could not be alienated except by release, a form of conveyance which could only operate in favour of a person already having some estate in the land. We h referred to the latest statement of the law on the point.
We now refer to one of the earliest, Lampet's Case(3). In thate (1) 3 C.B.N.S., 338, (3) 10 Reps, 460. OLR.) OF AUSTRALIA.

Blizabeth. Elizabeth in the lifetime of John Morrice joined im Morrice's estate for life was in law greater than the term of vs, and her estate was contingent upon his death before the jase was determined, an event which might or might not happen. Jewas contended that she had nothing but a possibility, but it «otherwise adjudged, and it was held that the effect of the zeae was to make John Morrice's estate absolute during all the 'sidue of the term.

We shall have occasion to refer to the case greater length for other purposes. We mention it now for the | of citing a passage at p. 49: "Tittleton saith that it isa maxim in law, that land in fee'simple, &., may be charged by one way or other : so it was said, that it was a maxim in law, that every right or title, or interest, inpresenti or futuro, by the joining of all who may claim any 'sath right, title, or interest, may be barred or extinguished, and herefore upon the maxim which Littleton puts, it was concluded, that if at the common law the donor and donee in tail had joined ina grant of a rent-charge, and afterwards the donee had died "without issue, and the land had reverted to the donor, he should Iuld it charged, and yet he had but a possibility at the time of 'the charge made: But all those who had estate or interest in 'pntsenti or futuro, joined in the charge: . . . So upon the d maxim, if in the case at bar John Morrice, the elder, and Elizabeth had joined in a deed of assignment to another, ithout question it had utterly barred the said Elizabeth, for no tiher had interest either in praesenti or futuro, but those who 'joined in the grant.

So when the husband of Elizabeth releases tohim in possession, both consented to it, one in releasing, the wther in accepting of it: And in the case when both join in the 'unt, it is the grant of him who has the term, and the release or imation of the other." 'The practical effect of the release was, therfore, to enlarge the estate of the tenant for life. These uthorities sufficiently establish that the beneficial interest in a 'entingent remainder was capable under some circumstances of assigned. But it was supposed to be settled that the z bi use of John Morrice for life with remainder to the testator's H.C. or A. 1905, Cananer Liovp, HIGH COURT * assignment could only be made by release. 'The rules ay, persons to whom a release of a right in land eould be made are stated in Littleton (Oo.

Litt., 265b.) "Also, in rg of all the right which a man hath in certain lands, &e. it him to whom the release is made in any ease, that he ha freehold in the lands in deed, or in law, at the time of the rk made &e. For in every case where he to whom the rele made hath the freehold in deed, or in law, at the time of release We,, there the release is good" (see. 447), "But a snyy cE them is good" (see. 450). It appears, then John Edwin's contingent remainder was prima facie ea being released to Michael, either regarded as tenant for remainder expectant on his mother's decease, or as joint remainder under the residuary de P But it was said that a contingent remainder could not b assigned to a stranger, exeept by a fine, or, in equity, by a tract for valuable consideration.

See the ease mention Fulwood's Case (1): "A man possessed of a term for di years, devised the profits thereof to one for life, and ater devisee entered by the assent of the executor, and afte in remainder during the life of the first devisee assigned i another, and afterwards the first devise died; it was adj that the assignment was void, for he in remainder had h possibility during the life of the first devise." differed from Lampet's Case in that the attempted alienation m by way of assignment to a stranger instead of by release to th tenant for life. Hurton (1), and in certain events had active duties to reform. It was suggested that the operative words of this deed are not sufficient to pass the contingent 'remainder in question.

As to the intention of the parties, there tan be no doubt that it was intended to include in the settlement all John Edwin's interests under his father's will, of whatever Kind. The word "release" which is used in it is the apt word for ialing with a contingent remainder, The Courts, so far from ring astute to defeat the intention of the parties, will endeavour {to construe the words used that that intention clearly expressed may prevail. 'Thus, in an assurance from one joint tenant to wother the word "grant" has been construed as " release," wise the only effectual mode of conveyance between such faties was by release, and it was the evident intention of the Iarties that the deed should have the effect of a conveyance. In 'ur judgment, therefore, John Edwin's contingent remainder now question was effectually released to Michael by the settlement.

CLR) OF AUSTRALIA. onmon sense, to create or raise an estate tail to a man upon the und of a limitation (viz. the freehold limited in trust) by which yo beneficial interest at all was intended to him." On this, Mr.

Butler remarks in a note: "With deference to Mr. Fearne —as Courts of law cannot take notice of any trusts charged on legal dates, the trusts or purposes for which the ancestor's estate of -frehold, in the eases proposed by him, is charged, cannot be a subject of their consideration, Courts of law, therefore, must treat the case merely as a limitation of a legal freehold to the ancestor, anda limitation of the legal fee to the'heirs of his body, and of 'course hold it to be a legal estate tail under the rule in Shelley's Case. With deference to the learned Judge from whom we are differing, it seems to us that the question of ownership of the legal estate is one thing and the obligations attaching to that ovnership are another.

We are concerned here, first, to ascertain the legal ownership, as a matter of ancient conveyancing law, of "John Edwin's undivided one-seventh share in Michael's moiety inremainder. And we adopt Mr, Butler's words: " As Courts of Jay eannot take notice of any trusts charged on legal estates, the trsts or purposes for which the . . . estate . . . is hanged cannot be a subject of their consideration." The doctrine yasa purely legal one, and we do not know of any equitable uetrine that forbids a person who is capable of acquiring a legal "state in land from undertaking to hold it on any trusts he thinks fit, or that requires a Court of Equity to refrain from enforcing such trusts against him. We do not think it is accurate to say that the release to Michael was conditional upon his holding the Ind on the trusts of the settlement. 'The release is in terms absolute, and a trust cannot properly be described as a condition. Ifthe deed of 1882 had been a bare release to Michael and his wits, and he had thereupon executed a covenant to stand seised on the trusts contained in it, there could have been no doubt of the validity of the trusts, and the fact that they are contained in the same instrument cannot make any difference.

Weare therefore of opinion that the settlement was effectual to Ms the interest in question to Michael, and that the accretion or jtcems absurd and inconsistent with any possible rule of Jaw or H©. ov A. 1905, ws Cananen Lioyp. In Doe Oliver (3), it was held that a fine come ceo &c., levied by con tingent remainderman in fee, operated by estoppel only, until th } contingency happened, and then operated to vest the estate absolutely in the cestui que use of the fine. The same point, have seen, has been decided as to demises by indenture; and the () 1H. BL, 30; 3 TLR, 88, (2) 2 Ed., 268. (3) 10 B. & C., 181. Inthe same note the learned author, referring to the question ofthe release of contingent remainders, says (p. 226):—"If the contingent interest in such a ease be sold to the person in whom the vested fee resides, it may be released; unless, indeed, according to the doctrine of some writers, such interests can only be devised or released where ' the owner is ascertained': see Prest.

Abstr, 283; but the fallacy of such a doctrine has been attempted {o be shown in a recent publication by the editor: see 1 Jar. Pow. Tex, 30,n, If the objection be applicable at all, it would apply to the present case (which indeed is admitted by Mr.

Preston in another place: 3 Prest. Abstr., 255), for it is uncertain, during the life of A., whether any, or, if any, which of the children will become an object of the gift. Such an interest is clearly not devisable, for the same reason that it is not descendible, namely, because it cannot subsist as such after the death of the owner; for ifa child survived A., the parent, the interest would vest, and ithe died in A's lifetime, it would fail; so that the devise could ever operate upon the property as a contingent interest.

This principle, however, does not apply to conveyances inter vivos} 'ind therefore, if there be no other reason why such an interest is tot devisable, it follows that it may be released; and such, it is 'Apprehended, is the sound conclusion upon the subject." _ An attentive consideration of the leading case of Doe v. Oliver 2) referred to by Mr. Jarman in the first part of the note just {uoted, will show that there is a great deal to be said in favour ithe view which he takes.

With regard to the second devise, Walker J. held that th settlement of 20th January, 1882, operated as a forfeiture John Edwin's life estate, and that the intermediate rents profits, until one of his children attained twenty-one, fell in residue.

On this point the decision is not appealed from, the learned Judge held that the interest of John Edwin residuary devisee quoad this property was merely the chance of his life estate being forfeited before any of his children hal attained a vested interest, (which is no doubt true), and that th interest was consequently contingent and not vested, and did ni pass by the settlement. Massey (2), which decided that the interest of a residuary devisee is vested and mt contingent, although it may depend upon the happening of eo tingencies whether the residuary devisee will ever take anything under the devise, was not cited to the learned Judge. If it had been, his decision on this point would doubtless have been the other way.

There can be no doubt of the sufficiency of th settlement to assign all the vested interests of the settlor, The appeal must therefore be allowed on this point also.
